---
title: "My Title"
shorttitle: "Response to Reviewers"
# Turn this document into a response-to-reviewers letter:
document: review
# Optional overrides:
# review-title: "Response to Reviewers"   # big heading on page 1
# review-response-label: "Response"       # lead-in label inside .response blocks
author:
  - name: Jane Doe
    corresponding: true
    email: janedoe@generic.edu
    affiliations:
      - name: Generic University
        department: Department of Scholarly Studies
        city: New York
        region: NY
        country: USA
abstract: ""
bibliography: bibliography.bib
format:
  apaquarto-pdf: default
  apaquarto-html: default
  apaquarto-docx: default
---

We thank the Editor and the reviewers for their careful reading of our
manuscript and for their constructive comments. Below we respond to each point
in turn. Reviewer and editor comments are shown in blue boxes, our responses in
green, and quoted changes to the revised manuscript in orange.

# Editor

::: {.editor}
Thank you for submitting your manuscript. Both reviewers see merit in the work
but raise several points that must be addressed before it can be accepted.
:::

::: {.response}
We are grateful for the opportunity to revise our manuscript. We have addressed
all of the points raised, as detailed below.
:::

# Associate Editor

::: {.editor}
Please pay particular attention to Reviewer 2's comment about the statistical
analysis.
:::

::: {.response}
We have revised the analysis accordingly; see our reply to Reviewer 2, Major
comment 1.
:::

# Reviewer 1

## Major comments

::: {.reviewer}
The theoretical framing in the Introduction could be clearer. It is not obvious
how the second hypothesis follows from the literature reviewed.
:::

::: {.response}
We have rewritten the final two paragraphs of the Introduction to make the
derivation of Hypothesis 2 explicit (pp. 3--4). The revised passage now reads:
:::

::: {.modification}
Building on this body of work, we therefore expected that participants in the
high-load condition would show the opposite pattern (Hypothesis 2), because the
mechanism outlined above predicts a reversal under cognitive load.
:::

## Minor comments

::: {.reviewer}
There is a typo on page 7, second paragraph.
:::

::: {.response}
Corrected, thank you.
:::

---

# Reviewer 2

::: {.reviewer}
The choice of statistical model is not justified. Why a linear model rather than
a mixed-effects model given the nested data?
:::

::: {.response}
This is an excellent point. We have re-run the analysis using a mixed-effects
model with random intercepts for site (Results, p. 9). The substantive
conclusions are unchanged.
:::

::: {.reviewer}
The sample size justification is missing.
:::

::: {.response}
We have added an a priori power analysis to the Method section (p. 6).
:::

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
